content.problemStatement:
Devus are creatures living in Devupur. There are total **n** Devus in Devupur. **ith** Devu live in **ith** house. Houses **1, 2, 3, , ,  n** are placed in a single lane i.e. **1** is a neighbor of **2**,  **2** is a neighbor of **1** and **3** etc.



They decide their president by a very weird voting system. All the Devus contest in the presidential elections. Each person uniformly randomly votes any of the **n** person as his preference (he can vote himself too). Devus are really very envious creatures, so no Devu casts his vote to the person whom his neighbor has casted. Devus vote in the increasing order of their IDs.



Finally all the votes are counted and all the Devus having votes equal to highest voted Devu are selected as president.



Now you are interested in finding out expected number of presidents Devupur can select in their upcoming election. Please round the answer to 6 digits after decimal.
content.inputFormat: First line contains T, the number of test cases. Each test case contains a single integer N.
content.outputFormat: For each test case, print the expected number of presidents Devupur can select in their upcoming election, rounded to 6 digits after decimal.

sampleTestCases.explanation: For the first test case, there is only one Devu, so the expected number of presidents is 1. For the second test case, there are two Devus, and each Devu can vote for the other Devu, so the expected number of presidents is 2.
For this test case, there are three Devus. Each Devu can vote for one of the other two Devus, so the expected number of presidents is 2.
For this test case, there are four Devus. Each Devu can vote for one of the other three Devus, so the expected number of presidents is 2.

solution.approach: The problem can be solved using dynamic programming. We can create a 2D array dp where dp[i][j] represents the probability that the ith Devu gets j votes. We can then calculate the expected number of presidents by summing up the probabilities of each Devu getting the maximum number of votes.
solution.timeComplexity: O(T * N^3) per test case, O(T * N^3) total.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(N^2) — the dominant structure is the 2D array dp.
